step2_rag_chunks: Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
Cravings for tobacco are temporary and usually last between 5 and 10 minutes. When a craving occurs, delay smoking and focus on another activity until the urge passes.
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.

step2_rag_chunks: It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.
Withdrawal symptoms usually peak within the first few days after quitting and gradually decrease over time.

step2_rag_chunks: Exploring why a patient wants to quit â€” whether for health, family, finances, or personal reasons â€” helps build intrinsic motivation that sustains the quit attempt during difficult moments. The stronger and more personal the reason, the more effective it is as a motivational anchor.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?
Support from friends, family, or healthcare professionals can improve the chances of successfully quitting smoking.

step2_rag_chunks: Talking to someone about your cravings can help reduce the intensity of the urge.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
